title: AI Ethics: Right and Wrong

modules.title: What Makes an AI Decision Ethical?
modules.sub: Exploring the values baked into every AI choice
modules.description: Learners examine what it means for a decision made by an algorithm to be considered ethical, fair, or just. Through real-world scenarios, they practice distinguishing between technical outputs and the moral assumptions driving them.

modules.title: Who Builds AI and Why It Matters
modules.sub: Understanding the people and power behind AI systems
modules.description: Learners investigate who designs, funds, and deploys AI technologies and how those identities and incentives shape outcomes. They reflect on how representation — or its absence — in AI development affects the systems we all live with.

modules.title: Bias: When Algorithms Get It Wrong
modules.sub: Recognizing how bias enters and harms AI systems
modules.description: Learners explore documented cases where AI systems produced biased or discriminatory results, tracing how bias enters through data, design, and deployment. They develop a working vocabulary for identifying bias and its real-world consequences.

modules.title: Privacy, Consent, and Your Data
modules.sub: Weighing personal data rights against AI's appetite for information
modules.description: Learners examine how AI systems collect, use, and monetize personal data, and what meaningful consent actually looks like in practice. They evaluate current norms and consider what stronger privacy protections would require from both individuals and institutions.

modules.title: Accountability: Who Is Responsible?
modules.sub: Tracing responsibility when AI causes harm
modules.description: Learners work through case studies in which AI-driven decisions led to harmful outcomes, asking who — developer, deployer, or user — should bear responsibility. They assess existing accountability gaps and explore what governance structures could close them.

modules.title: AI and Human Rights
modules.sub: Connecting AI impacts to fundamental rights and dignity
modules.description: Learners map how AI deployment intersects with recognized human rights, including freedom of expression, equality, and due process. They analyze situations where AI has amplified or undermined those rights and consider what a rights-based approach to AI would demand.

modules.title: Ethical Frameworks for AI Choices
modules.sub: Applying moral philosophy to real AI dilemmas
modules.description: Learners are introduced to major ethical frameworks — including utilitarianism, deontology, and virtue ethics — and apply each to concrete AI dilemmas. They practice using these lenses to reason through trade-offs rather than arriving at easy answers.

modules.title: Becoming an Ethical AI Citizen
modules.sub: Turning ethical awareness into everyday action and advocacy
modules.description: Learners synthesize insights from the course into a personal stance on AI ethics and identify practical steps they can take as consumers, employees, and community members. They leave with a framework for continued critical engagement as AI technology evolves.

synopsis: This course introduces everyday people to the moral questions shaping AI development, from bias and privacy to accountability and human rights. No technical background needed — just curiosity about the world AI is building around us.
rationale: With a demand signal of 7/10 and low corpus similarity of 0.61 relative to the nearest course 'AI and Democracy', there is a clear gap for an accessible, ethics-first course that addresses foundational moral reasoning rather than political structures alone.
